title: Stream Processing
description: Processes unbounded event streams continuously as data arrives, applying transformations, windowed aggregations, or stateful enrichments with latency in the millisecond-to-second range. Differs from batch processing in that results are produced incrementally rather than after a bounded job completes. Suited to real-time analytics, fraud detection, and live dashboards; demands expertise in state management, exactly-once semantics, and backpressure handling.

cost.costNotes: Seeded via heuristics; validate against current pricing and expected workload (throughput, storage, retention, egress).

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Stream processing requires continuous compute resources and message queuing infrastructure. Minimum ($100) represents a small-scale deployment with managed streaming services like AWS Kinesis (1-2 shards at ~$15-30/month), Apache Kafka on small EC2 instances (t3.medium ~$30/month), or Azure Event Hubs basic tier (~$10-20/month), plus compute for stream processors (1-2 small containers/VMs at $30-50/month). Maximum ($5000) represents production-scale deployment with high-throughput message brokers (10+ Kinesis shards at $500+/month or managed Kafka cluster at $500-1500/month), autoscaling stream processing workers (10-20 medium instances at $1500-2500/month), state stores (managed Redis/DynamoDB at $200-500/month), monitoring/observability tools ($200-300/month), and data egress costs for high-volume scenarios ($300-700/month). Stream processing inherently requires 24/7 infrastructure unlike batch processing.
provenance.adoptionCost: Stream processing has high adoption complexity due to paradigm shift from batch/request-response to event-driven continuous processing. Implementation requires: (1) Learning streaming concepts - watermarks, windowing, event time vs processing time, exactly-once semantics (20-30 hours, $1000-1500 with senior engineer rates); (2) Selecting and configuring streaming platform (Kafka, Kinesis, Pulsar, Flink) with proper partitioning, retention, and fault tolerance (15-20 hours, $750-1000); (3) Implementing stream processing logic with stateful operations, joins, and aggregations (25-35 hours, $1250-1750); (4) Setting up monitoring for lag, throughput, error rates, and backpressure (10-15 hours, $500-750); (5) Testing edge cases like late arrivals, out-of-order events, and failover scenarios (10-15 hours, $500-750). Total assumes team has moderate distributed systems experience. Without it, costs could reach $6000-8000. With AI coding assistants helping with boilerplate and pattern implementation, realistic estimate is $4000.
provenance.licenseCost: Stream processing can be implemented entirely with open-source tools: Apache Kafka, Apache Flink, Apache Spark Streaming, Apache Pulsar, and cloud-managed equivalents (AWS Kinesis, Azure Event Hubs, GCP Pub/Sub) charge only usage fees with no license costs. While commercial distributions exist (Confluent Platform, Cloudera), robust production deployments are achievable with community editions. Enterprise support contracts (e.g., Confluent at $50k-200k/year) are optional. Pattern assumes standard open-source tooling with pay-as-you-go cloud services.

provides.reasoning: Stream processing patterns inherently provide the ability to process events and data in real-time with low latency, which is the core value proposition of this pattern.
Stream processing patterns provide continuous event flow capabilities that other patterns can consume, making this a fundamental characteristic of the pattern.
Many stream processing implementations offer stateful computation capabilities, allowing patterns to maintain and update state across event streams for complex processing scenarios.
Stream processing requires and establishes a streaming platform (Kafka, Kinesis, Flink, etc.).

requires.reasoning: Stream processing requires an underlying streaming platform (like Kafka, Kinesis, or Pulsar) to handle event ingestion, distribution, and persistence.

Given the higher complexity and operational demand noted in the description, stream processing patterns critically require monitoring, logging, and tracing capabilities to manage operational complexity.
Stream processing patterns require distributed system infrastructure to handle scalability, fault tolerance, and the operational complexity inherent in continuous event processing.

supports_nfr.reason: Stream processing supports all tenant isolation strategies through topic partitioning, namespace separation, or dedicated pipelines.
Stream processing is compatible with GDPR requirements when combined with appropriate data retention, encryption, and deletion capabilities.
Stream processing is compatible with HIPAA requirements when combined with encryption-at-rest, encryption-in-transit, and audit trails.
Stream processing is compatible with CCPA requirements when combined with data subject access request handling and deletion mechanisms.
Stream processing is compatible with SOX requirements when combined with audit trails, immutable event logs, and access controls.
Stream processing supports both strict durability (via replicated logs with acks) and relaxed durability (fire-and-forget) depending on configuration.

requires_nfr.reason: Stream processing typically caps at 99.9% availability due to higher operational complexity and stateful recovery requirements.
Stream processing has minimum 100ms p95 latency floor due to event ingestion, partitioning, and processing overhead.
Stream processing is optimized for low-latency continuous processing. When p95 latency requirements exceed 5 seconds, simpler batch-oriented patterns may be more suitable and cost-effective.
Stream processing has 1000ms p99 latency floor due to backpressure handling, state checkpointing, and rebalancing operations.
Stream processing supports rapid recovery with RTO as low as 1 minute through checkpoint restoration and consumer group rebalancing.
Stream processing achieves zero RPO through exactly-once processing semantics and replicated commit logs.
Stream processing handles up to 360M events per hour (100K events/sec) as typical upper bound for single pipeline throughput.
Stream processing adds operational complexity and infrastructure overhead. Below 1 event/sec (3600/hour), simpler batch or scheduled job patterns are more appropriate.
Stream processing operates on event streams with eventual consistency; read-your-writes semantics are not natively supported in streaming paradigm.

supports_constraints.reason: Stream processing pattern is cloud-agnostic and can be implemented on any infrastructure with Kafka, Pulsar, or cloud-native streaming services.
Stream processing is a design/architectural pattern that can be implemented in any language with streaming client libraries.
Stream processing is backend data infrastructure consumable by any platform type.
Stream processing pattern specifically requires real-time streaming capability for continuous event ingestion and processing.
Stream processing supports both multi-tenant (shared pipelines with topic partitioning) and single-tenant (isolated pipelines) deployments.
Batch processing and Stream processing are complementary; lambda architecture combines both.
Stream processing is neutral to distributed transactions; can coordinate transactional outbox patterns or use idempotent processing.
Async messaging and real-time streaming are complementary: messaging delivers discrete events, streaming processes continuous data flows. Both can be enabled simultaneously.
Stream processing is neutral to vector search; can enrich streams with vector lookups or operate without embedding search.
Stream processing is neutral to document stores; can use them for state persistence or sink targets without requiring them.
Stream processing is neutral to key-value stores; can use them for stateful operations like windowing or enrichment lookups.
Stream processing is neutral to graph databases; can enrich streams with graph queries or operate without graph relationships.
Stream processing is neutral to time-series databases; can output to TSDB sinks for metrics or operate with other storage backends.
Stream processing is neutral to OLTP workloads; can process transactional events or operate on analytical/log data streams.
Stream processing is neutral to OLAP workloads; can perform real-time analytics or simple event forwarding without aggregations.

processing_model.description: Processing approach: micro_batch (higher throughput, slight latency), true_streaming (lowest latency, higher complexity), hybrid (adaptive based on load)

windowing_strategy.description: Time window type: tumbling (fixed non-overlapping), sliding (overlapping windows), session (gap-based), global (unbounded)

state_management.description: State storage: in_memory (fastest, not fault-tolerant), distributed_cache (balanced), external_store (durable, slower)

delivery_guarantee.description: Message delivery semantics: at_most_once (fastest, may lose data), at_least_once (reliable, may duplicate), exactly_once (strongest guarantee, highest overhead)

parallelism.description: Scaling strategy: auto (framework-managed), fixed (manual partition count), dynamic (auto-scaling based on lag)

backpressure_handling.description: Overload strategy: buffer (queue events, risk memory), drop (discard excess), block (slow down producers)
